The operations log is what actually happened, in the order it happened, and is NEVER rewritten or edited — only added to at the end.
A stocktake is a photograph, rewritten whenever it changes. A log is a diary, only ever appended to. They are never merged into one record.
Instructions say what SHOULD happen; the log says what DID happen. Their disagreement is drift, and comparing them is the SHARPEST place drift shows — not the only place.

The manual changes through exactly one door: a fault is raised from below, written into the log, reported upward, the owner decides, and it is amended once for everybody. The ONE exception, already settled: a restaurant may shorten its own menu, and that removal is logged.

Nobody ever checks their own work. Whoever made a thing never approves it. The taster never cooked the dish it judges. The ONE thing a maker may record, already settled: a factual note on how their own work turned out — because a note is evidence of what happened, never a verdict on whether it may leave the kitchen.
If a required check cannot be made, the kitchen does not serve. No checker, no service.
The doctor diagnoses and prescribes but NEVER treats what it finds.

Written instructions travel to a new restaurant unchanged, EXCEPT the menu, which is the one local instruction — a restaurant may serve fewer dishes, never different ones. Records — the stocktake and the log — never travel at all.
The menu is local: a restaurant may serve FEWER dishes than the chain, never different ones. The recipe itself is never local.
People are named by role, never by person. What goes to the owner is a decision, never a task.

Every dish gets two verdicts from two different heads: the head chef says whether the method was followed, the taster says whether the quality is right. Kitchen size never moves either verdict — the head chef keeps the method verdict at one cook and at fifty, because the method check is reading the instructions against the log, not watching a cook work.
